summary: Gruber reacts to The Information's report on Apple's upcoming AR/VR headset, questioning several reported features including the external tethered battery pack, the $3,000 price point, and the outward-facing display showing the wearer's facial expressions. He pushes back on the framing that the headset needs iPhone-level success to be worthwhile, arguing that iPad or Apple Watch-level revenue would be a huge win. He's skeptical of the reported killer apps like videoconferencing and 3D maps, and notes contradictions in the report about gaming focus. He concludes that the fundamental question remains unanswered: what is the compelling use case that justifies the hassle and cost of strapping on a headset?
key_points: Not every Apple product needs to be an iPhone-sized success — iPad, Apple Watch, and AirPods are all hits without matching iPhone revenue
An external tethered battery pack contradicts the trend toward seamless, dip-in-dip-out personal computing and could be a deal-breaker
A $3,000 price point wouldn't kill the product but signals the platform is years away from large-scale adoption
The reported killer apps (videoconferencing, 3D maps, educational content) are unconvincing justifications for a $3,000 headset
The headset must prove it does some tasks a lot better than iPhone or Mac — the same bar the iPad had to clear
money_quotes: I don't know why every possible new product from Apple is always framed as though it needs to be an iPhone-esque success or it's a bust.
The history of personal computing has been in the direction of ever more seamless dipping in and out when using new devices.
Either Ma is wrong, Apple is about to jump the shark, or this device is going to be so utterly compelling that a tethered battery is worth the hassle.
Facebook's failures on the VR headset front are no more reason to be cautious than, say, all the shitty tablets PC makers tried to sell before the iPad.
No one is going to buy a $3,000 headset for videoconferencing.
No one is going to buy a $3,000 headset for looking at 3D tabletop maps.
If it didn't serve some tasks not just a little but a lot better than either an iPhone or Mac, there was no point to the iPad. The same is true for this headset.
If it costs $3,000 and/or requires a tethered battery strapped around your waist, the 'this better be an awesome experience' bar is raised even higher.
key_insight: Apple's headset must clear the same bar as the iPad — proving it does specific tasks dramatically better than existing devices — and the reported $3,000 price and tethered battery only raise that bar higher.
